chunks.text: Big-O Notation Prof. Timothy Sun Big-O Notation “Insertion sort runs in O(n2) time.” Why use Big-O notation? ● Language/machine/unit-independent ● Isolating the “bottleneck” ● Captures long-term behavior ● Easier to analyze Why use Big-O notation? ● Language/machine/unit-independent ● Isolating the “bottleneck” ● Captures long-term behavior ● Easier to analyze Big-O notation is about scalability, not speed! Asymptotes Big-O notation O(f(n)): class of functions whose “order is at most f(n).” O(f(n)): class of functions whose “order is at most f(n).” Big-O notation Big-O notation O(f(n)): class of functions whose “order is at most f(n).” More often you’ll see it (technically, incorrectly) written as: Deﬁnition: f(x) = O(g(x)), i.e., “f(x) is order at most g(x)”, if: There exist numbers C, K > 0 so that f(x) ≤ C·g(x), for every x ≥ K. Big-O notation Deﬁnition: f(x) = O(g(x)), i.e., “f(x) is order at most g(x)”, if: There exist numbers C, K > 0 so that f(x) ≤ C·g(x), for every x ≥ K. Big-O notation C: use it to hide the coeﬃcient K: only care about “big enough” x Some examples: ● 3x + 4 = O(x) ● 4x2 + 10x - 21 = O(x2) ● 81x = O(x3) ● x2 ≠ O(x) ● log2 x = O(ln x) [base doesn’t matter] ● x100 = O(2x) ● 2n = O(n!) Big-O notation One approach is to “guess” C, then ﬁnd K algebraically: e.g. 3x + 4 = O(x) A linear example One approach is to “guess” C, then ﬁnd K algebraically: e.g. 3x + 4 = O(x) 1. “Guess” a C bigger than 3: try C = 4 A linear example One approach is to “guess” C, then ﬁnd K algebraically: e.g. 3x + 4 = O(x) 1. “Guess” a C bigger than 3: try C = 4 2. Solve for K: 3n + 4 ≤ 4x → x ≥ 4, so K can be 4. A linear example One approach is to “guess” C, then ﬁnd K algebraically: e.g. 3x + 4 = O(x) 1. “Guess” a C bigger than 3: try C = 4 2. Solve for K: 3n + 4 ≤ 4x → x ≥ 4, so K can be 4. In general, many pairs of C and K can work. A linear example 4x2 +10x - 21 = O(x2) A quadratic example 4x2 +10x - 21 = O(x2) 1. Guess C = 5. A quadratic example 4x2 +10x - 21 = O(x2) 1. Guess C = 5. 2. Solve for K: 4x2 + 10x - 21 ≤ 5x2 → 0 ≤ x2 - 10x + 21 = (x - 3)(x - 7) A quadratic example 4x2 +10x - 21 = O(x2) 1. Guess C = 5. 2. Solve for K: 4x2 + 10x - 21 ≤ 5x2 → 0 ≤ x2 - 10x + 21 = (x - 3)(x - 7) K = 7 works, but not K = 3 (why?) A quadratic example Logarithms under different bases are related by the change of base formula: loga x = logb x / logb x = (1 / logb a) logb x Logarithms Logarithms under different bases are related by the change of base formula: loga x = logb x / logb x = (1 / logb a) logb x Inside of big-O notation, ignore the base: O(log

- 3)(x - 7) K = 7 works, but not K = 3 (why?) A quadratic example Logarithms under different bases are related by the change of base formula: loga x = logb x / logb x = (1 / logb a) logb x Logarithms Logarithms under different bases are related by the change of base formula: loga x = logb x / logb x = (1 / logb a) logb x Inside of big-O notation, ignore the base: O(log x), not O(log2 x) Logarithms Logarithms under different bases are related by the change of base formula: loga x = logb x / logb x = (1 / logb a) logb x Inside of big-O notation, ignore the base: O(log x), not O(log2 x) (unless it appears as an exponent) Logarithms For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 General themes For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 = O(x5) General themes Simple approach for polynomials 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 Simple approach for polynomials 1. Remove negative terms 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x4 + x2 + 11 true for x ≥ 0 Simple approach for polynomials 1. Remove negative terms 2. Raise to highest power 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x4 + x2 + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x5 + x5 + 11x5 true for x ≥ 0 true for x ≥ 1 Simple approach for polynomials 1. Remove negative terms 2. Raise to highest power 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x4 + x2 + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x5 + x5 + 11x5 = 21x5 true for x ≥ 0 true for x ≥ 1 Simple approach for polynomials 1. Remove negative terms 2. Raise to highest power 3x5 + 6x4 - 10x3 + x2 - 15x + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x4 + x2 + 11 ≤ 3x5 + 6x5 + x5 + 11x5 = 21x5 C true for x ≥ 0 true for x ≥ 1 K = 1 Polynomials vs. Exponentials One can interpret big-O notation as a limit f(x)/g(x) ≤ C, for every x ≥ K. Polynomials vs. Exponentials One can interpret big-O notation as a limit f(x)/g(x) ≤ C, for every x ≥ K. Can use calculus to help evaluate things like x100 = O(ex). Polynomials vs. Exponentials One can interpret big-O notation as a limit f(x)/g(x) ≤ C, for every x ≥ K. Can use calculus to help evaluate things like x100 = O(ex). For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) General themes For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) constant < logarithmic < polynomial < exponential General themes For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) constant < logarithmic < polynomial < exponential

drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) General themes For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) constant < logarithmic < polynomial < exponential General themes For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) constant < logarithmic < polynomial < exponential logb n = O(nd), for any b > 1, d > 0 General themes For polynomials, look at the highest power, drop the coeﬃcient. 203n5 + 92n4 - 1423n3 + n2 + 86n - 571 = O(n5) constant < logarithmic < polynomial < exponential logb n = O(nd), for any b > 1, d > 0 nd = O(cn) for any d > 0, c > 1. General themes na = O(nb), if a ≤ b Useful properties na = O(nb), if a ≤ b If f(n) = O(g(n)), and h(n) > 0, then f(n)h(n) = O(g(n)h(n)) Useful properties na = O(nb), if a ≤ b If f(n) = O(g(n)), and h(n) > 0, then f(n)h(n) = O(g(n)h(n)) n = O(n log n), n log n = O(n2), O(n2) = O(n3), etc. Useful properties “O(f(n))+O(g(n)) = O(f(n)+g(n))” Useful properties “O(f(n))+O(g(n)) = O(f(n)+g(n))” ● If a(n) = O(f(n)) and b(n) = O(g(n)), then a(n) + b(n) = O(f(n) + g(n)) Useful properties “O(f(n))O(g(n)) = O(f(n)g(n))” ● If a(n) = O(f(n)) and b(n) = O(g(n)), then a(n)b(n) = O(f(n)g(n)) Useful when considering the complexity of two distinct steps in an algorithm. Useful properties ● O(f(n)) + O(g(n)) = O(f(n) + g(n)) ● O(f(n))O(g(n)) = O(f(n)g(n)) ● O(a·f(n)) = O(f(n)), for constant a > 0. ● f(n) = O(g(n)), g(n) = O(h(n)) → f(n) = O(h(n)). ● O(f(n)+g(n)) = O(max(f(n), g(n))) Useful properties Give a good big-O estimate of f(n) using a simple function f(n) = 20 (n2 + n2 log4 n)(4n + 3) + (17 log3 n + 19)(n2 + 2)(ln n - 3) Simplifying expressions Give a good big-O estimate of f(n) using a simple function f(n) = 20 (n2 + n2 log4 n)(4n + 3) + (17 log3 n + 19)(n2 + 2)(ln n - 3) = O(n2 log n)O(n) + O(log n)O(n2)O(log n) = O(n3 log n) + O(n2(log n)2) = O(n3 log n) Simplifying expressions f(x) = Ω(g(x)) if there are numbers C, K > 0 so that f(x) ≥ C·g(x), whenever x ≥ K. It’s all Greek to me f(x) = Ω(g(x)) if there are numbers C, K > 0 so that f(x) ≥ C·g(x), whenever x ≥ K. x3 - 3x + 10 = Ω(x2) It’s all Greek to me f(x) = Ω(g(x)) if there are numbers C, K > 0 so that f(x) ≥ C·g(x), whenever x ≥ K. x3 - 3x + 10 = Ω(x2) (could also think of as g = O(f)) It’s all Greek to me f(x) = ϴ(g(x)) if there are numbers C1, C2, K > 0 so that C1g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ C2g(x), whenever x ≥ K. It’s all Greek to me f(x) = ϴ(g(x)) if there are numbers C1, C2, K > 0 so that C1g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ C2g(x), whenever x ≥ K. (can also think of as

whenever x ≥ K. x3 - 3x + 10 = Ω(x2) (could also think of as g = O(f)) It’s all Greek to me f(x) = ϴ(g(x)) if there are numbers C1, C2, K > 0 so that C1g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ C2g(x), whenever x ≥ K. It’s all Greek to me f(x) = ϴ(g(x)) if there are numbers C1, C2, K > 0 so that C1g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ C2g(x), whenever x ≥ K. (can also think of as f = O(g) and f = Ω(g)) It’s all Greek to me Let f(x) = x ln x, g(x) = x(x + 1)/2, h(x) = (x - 1)3 It’s all Greek to me Let f(x) = x ln x, g(x) = x(x + 1)/2, h(x) = (x - 1)3 ● f(x) = O(x2), g(x) = O(x2), h(x) ≠ O(x2) ● f(x) ≠ Ω(x2), g(x) = Ω(x2), h(x) = Ω(x2) It’s all Greek to me Let f(x) = x ln x, g(x) = x(x + 1)/2, h(x) = (x - 1)3 ● f(x) = O(x2), g(x) = O(x2), h(x) ≠ O(x2) ● f(x) ≠ Ω(x2), g(x) = Ω(x2), h(x) = Ω(x2) Putting these together, we get: ● f(x) ≠ ϴ(x2), g(x) = ϴ(x2), h(x) ≠ ϴ(x2) Only g(x) has quadratic growth. It’s all Greek to me O: “≤” Ω: “≥” ϴ: “=” It’s all Greek to me O: “≤” Ω: “≥” ϴ: “=” o: “<” ω: “>” It’s all Greek to me Concluding Remarks Big-O notation is useful for measuring the scalability of algorithms, independent of any implementation… Concluding Remarks Big-O notation is useful for measuring the scalability of algorithms, independent of any implementation… ...but it doesn’t always tell the whole story because of the hidden constant. ● e.g. mergesort (O(n log n)) vs. insertion sort (O(n2)) for small values. Concluding Remarks Big-O notation is useful for measuring the scalability of algorithms, independent of any implementation… ...but it doesn’t always tell the whole story because of the hidden constant. ● e.g. mergesort (O(n log n)) vs. insertion sort (O(n2)) for small values. Not a substitute for benchmarking! Concluding Remarks “Unfortunately, for any instance [graph] that one could ﬁt into the known universe, one would easily prefer n70 to even constant time, if that constant had to be one of Robertson and Seymour’s.” -David Johnson, The NP-Completeness Column: An Ongoing Guide (1987).
